**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I must confess that my hands are somewhat unsteady as I write. The wind howls like a beast upon the cliffs, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this coast. I have spent a lifetime watching the tides and the tempests, yet tonight, the storm seems to have a mind of its own, as though it seeks to test the very soul of a man.

We have not spoken in five years, and I have no doubt that you would think me a fool for writing, yet I must. There is something in the air tonight, a sense that the world is ending, and I feel I must speak to you before the end.

You were always a spirited lass, full of fire and ambition. I recall the days when you would race across the fields, your boots kicking up the dust, and your laughter ringing like a bell. I would have given you a stern tongue, yet I could not deny the light in your eyes.

I have spent many a night alone in this tower, watching the light dance upon the waves, and the stars blink like distant lanterns. Tonight, as the wind howls and the sea roars, I feel a kinship with the waves, as though they are whispering secrets to me. I have always believed that the sea holds the answers to all things, and I feel tonight, it may whisper

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I trust this missive finds you in good health, though I must confess I have not been in correspondence with you for nigh five years. Time, as you know, is a fickle thing, and I have found myself in a most disagreeable state of affairs, both in heart and in spirit. I write to you now not out of mere sentiment, but out of a most pressing need—this night a tempest of such ferocity is upon us, and I fear for my own survival.

You, my child, are a most precious thing in this world, and though our paths have diverged, I cannot help but think of you oft. I have seen many a soul in this world, and have been blessed with the grace of God to endure much, yet I find myself in a most dire situation. Should I not survive this night, I would not wish to leave you in sorrow, but I would rather have you know that I have done my duty as a father, as a keeper of this light, and as a man of God.

I have lit the lamp, and the light burns with a most steady flame. It is a symbol of hope, as you once said, and I take comfort in that. Should I not live to see the dawn, I would not wish for you to be left in darkness. I have done my part, and I have no regrets.
